{
  "file_path": "/home/gem/project/tests/regression/pwa.spec.ts",
  "content": "/**\n * @regression\n *\n * PWA installability and service worker tests.\n * Verifies manifest completeness, icon availability, and SW behaviour.\n */\n\nimport { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';\n\ntest.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.goto('/');\n});\n\n// \u2500\u2500 Manifest \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n\ntest('@regression manifest has required PWA fields', async ({ page }) => {\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/manifest.json');\n  expect(resp.ok()).toBe(true);\n  const m = await resp.json();\n\n  expect(m.name).toBeTruthy();\n  expect(m.short_name).toBeTruthy();\n  expect(m.start_url).toBeTruthy();\n  expect(m.display).toBe('standalone');\n  expect(m.background_color).toBeTruthy();\n  expect(m.theme_color).toBeTruthy();\n});\n\ntest('@regression manifest icons include 192 and 512 sizes', async ({ page }) => {\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/manifest.json');\n  const m = await resp.json();\n\n  const sizes = (m.icons as Array<{ sizes: string }>).map((i) => i.sizes);\n  expect(sizes.some((s) => s.includes('192'))).toBe(true);\n  expect(sizes.some((s) => s.includes('512'))).toBe(true);\n});\n\ntest('@regression manifest 192x192 icon is reachable', async ({ page }) => {\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/manifest.json');\n  const m = await resp.json();\n  const icon192 = (m.icons as Array<{ src: string; sizes: string }>).find((i) => i.sizes.includes('192'));\n  expect(icon192).toBeTruthy();\n  const iconResp = await page.request.get(`/${icon192!.src}`);\n  expect(iconResp.ok()).toBe(true);\n});\n\ntest('@regression manifest 512x512 icon is reachable', async ({ page }) => {\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/manifest.json');\n  const m = await resp.json();\n  const icon512 = (m.icons as Array<{ src: string; sizes: string }>).find((i) => i.sizes.includes('512'));\n  expect(icon512).toBeTruthy();\n  const iconResp = await page.request.get(`/${icon512!.src}`);\n  expect(iconResp.ok()).toBe(true);\n});\n\ntest('@regression manifest is linked in HTML head', async ({ page }) => {\n  const manifestLink = page.locator('link[rel=\"manifest\"]');\n  await expect(manifestLink).toHaveCount(1);\n  const href = await manifestLink.getAttribute('href');\n  expect(href).toContain('manifest.json');\n});\n\ntest('@regression theme-color meta matches manifest theme_color', async ({ page }) => {\n  const metaColor = await page.locator('meta[name=\"theme-color\"]').getAttribute('content');\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/manifest.json');\n  const m = await resp.json();\n  expect(metaColor?.toLowerCase()).toBe(m.theme_color.toLowerCase());\n});\n\n// \u2500\u2500 PWA installability criteria \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n\ntest('@regression page is served over HTTPS', async ({ page }) => {\n  const url = page.url();\n  expect(url.startsWith('https://')).toBe(true);\n});\n\ntest('@regression apple-mobile-web-app-capable meta is present', async ({ page }) => {\n  const meta = page.locator('meta[name=\"apple-mobile-web-app-capable\"]');\n  await expect(meta).toHaveCount(1);\n  await expect(meta).toHaveAttribute('content', 'yes');\n});\n\n// \u2500\u2500 Service worker \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n\ntest('@regression service worker script is accessible', async ({ page }) => {\n  const resp = await page.request.get('/sw.js');\n  expect(resp.ok()).toBe(true);\n});\n\ntest('@regression service worker scope covers the origin', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.waitForTimeout(1500); // allow SW to activate\n  const swInfo = await page.evaluate(async () => {\n    const regs = await navigator.serviceWorker.getRegistrations();\n    return regs.map((r) => ({ scope: r.scope }));\n  });\n  expect(swInfo.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);\n  // Scope should be the origin root\n  expect(swInfo[0].scope).toContain('hey-chill.vercel.app');\n});\n\ntest('@regression service worker is in activated state', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.waitForTimeout(1500);\n  const state = await page.evaluate(async () => {\n    const regs = await navigator.serviceWorker.getRegistrations();\n    return regs[0]?.active?.state ?? null;\n  });\n  expect(state).toBe('activated');\n});\n"
}

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #72
Write TOOL CALL 
{
  "file_path": "/home/gem/project/tests/regression/settings.spec.ts",
  "content": "/**\n * @regression\n *\n * Settings modal tests.\n * Covers open/close behaviour, webhook URL persistence, and keyboard nav.\n */\n\nimport { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';\nimport { SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT } from '../helpers/speech-mock';\n\ntest.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.addInitScript(SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT);\n  await page.goto('/');\n});\n\ntest('@regression settings modal opens on settings button click', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeVisible();\n});\n\ntest('@regression settings modal closes on X button click', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeVisible();\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Close settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n\ntest('@regression settings modal closes on Escape key', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeVisible();\n  await page.keyboard.press('Escape');\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n\ntest('@regression settings modal closes on overlay backdrop click', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeVisible();\n  // Click the overlay area outside the dialog box\n  await page.locator('#modal-overlay').click({ position: { x: 5, y: 5 } });\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL is saved to localStorage on Save', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  const stored = await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.getItem('webhookUrl'));\n  expect(stored).toBe(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression saved webhook URL is pre-populated when modal reopens', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  // Save\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  // Reopen\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.locator('#webhook-url')).toHaveValue(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL persists across page reload', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  // Reload\n  await page.reload();\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.locator('#webhook-url')).toHaveValue(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL can be cleared', async ({ page }) => {\n  // First set a URL\n  await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.setItem('webhookUrl', 'https://old.example.com'));\n  await page.reload();\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill('');\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  const stored = await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.getItem('webhookUrl'));\n  expect(stored).toBe('');\n});\n\ntest('@regression saving closes the modal', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n"
}

expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n\ntest('@regression settings modal closes on Escape key', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n overlay backdrop click', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeVisible();\n  // Click the overlay area outside the dialog box\n  await page.locator('#modal-overlay').click({ position: { x: 5, y: 5 } });\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL is saved to localStorage on Save', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  const stored = await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.getItem('webhookUrl'));\n  expect(stored).toBe(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression saved webhook URL is pre-populated when modal reopens', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  // Save\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  // Reopen\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.locator('#webhook-url')).toHaveValue(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL persists across page reload', async ({ page }) => {\n  const webhookUrl = 'https://n8n.example.com/webhook/hey-chill';\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill(webhookUrl);\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  // Reload\n  await page.reload();\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await expect(page.locator('#webhook-url')).toHaveValue(webhookUrl);\n});\n\ntest('@regression webhook URL can be cleared', async ({ page }) => {\n  // First set a URL\n  await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.setItem('webhookUrl', 'https://old.example.com'));\n  await page.reload();\n\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.locator('#webhook-url').fill('');\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n\n  const stored = await page.evaluate(() => localStorage.getItem('webhookUrl'));\n  expect(stored).toBe('');\n});\n\ntest('@regression saving closes the modal', async ({ page }) => {\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Settings' }).click();\n  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();\n  await expect(page.getByRole('dialog')).toBeHidden();\n});\n"
    },
    "caller": {
      "type": "direct"
    }
  }
}

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #83
There are failures. Let me analyze the root cause: 1. The push-to-talk tests that click the mic button fail - the recording state doesn't update. This is likely because the mic button click fires `startListening()` which calls `recognition.start()`, and our mock's `start()` method fires the 'start' event via a Promise. But the `recognition.onstart` handler isn't being set in my mock properly because `_fire` dispatches the event but the `onstart` handler should be set by the app code. Actually, wait - looking at the mock more carefully. The app sets: ```js recognition.onstart = () => { ... } ``` And the mock fires the event like: ```js _fire(name, detail) { const handler = this['on' + name]; if (typeof handler === 'function') handler.call(this, ev); ... } ``` But `_fire` is called with just the name and creates a generic `Event`. The issue is that `event.type` in a generic `new Event('start')` actually gets overridden by the code to set `ev.type = name`... wait that doesn't work. Let me look at the mock again. Examining the mock's event firing mechanism more closely - I think the problem is that when `_fire` is called, it's creating a generic Event object but the handler lookup using `this['on' + name]` should work. Let me trace through what happens when the app code sets `recognition.onstart` and then the mock calls `_fire('start')`. Actually, I'm realizing the Event type property is read-only in the browser, so trying to set `ev.type = name` after creating the Event won't work. But `new Event(name)` already sets the type correctly, so that assignment is either redundant or the mock needs to create the Event differently. Let me think about what the `start()` method is actually doing... It sets `_started` to true, then schedules the `_fire('start')` call as a microtask. The test clicks the mic button, which triggers `startListening()` and calls `recognition.start()`. This schedules the 'start' event to fire, which should set `isListening = true` and update the UI. But the test expects `aria-pressed` to be true immediately after the click, and there might be a timing issue where the microtask hasn't fired yet before the assertion runs. Now I'm looking at the VAD test failure. It's using `page.clock.install()` to control time, and the test is checking whether VAD auto-stops recording after 3500 ms of silence. The test clicks the mic button but seems to be cut off mid-execution. The core problem is that when the app's click handler calls `recognition.start()`, the mock fires the `onstart` handler asynchronously via a microtask, but the test assertion checks the button state synchronously before that microtask has a chance to run. So the `aria-pressed` attribute never gets set to `true` because the `onstart` handler hasn't executed yet. Looking at the actual test failures, they're timing out rather than failing on the assertion itself. The test is trying to click the mic button, but it seems the element isn't even visible or clickable when the test runs. Let me trace through what's happening in the test setup and execution to figure out why the button isn't in the right state when we try to interact with it. The mock's `start()` method fires the `onstart` handler as a microtask, which should trigger `setRecordingUI(true)` and update `aria-pressed` before the test assertion runs. The real issue might be the silence timer that gets set with a 3500ms delay, which could be interfering with the test's timing expectations. Looking more closely at the `_fire` method... Examining the event firing mechanism... The `_fire` method creates an event and dispatches it through `dispatchEvent`, which should work since the mock extends `EventTarget`. But I'm realizing the actual problem might be elsewhere in how the app code sets up the recognition listener. The app's `onresult` handler expects the event to have `resultIndex` and `results` properties, but the mock's `_emitResult` method tries to assign these directly to the Event object, which won't work since Event properties are read-only. So those properties never actually get set on the event that gets dispatched. Actually, wait—I can set custom properties on Event objects in JavaScript, they're not sealed. So that should work. Let me reconsider what's happening here. Maybe the issue is that `page.waitForTimeout(100)` isn't giving the mock enough time to fire, or the Promise microtask is getting lost somehow. Looking back at the VAD test failures, test 37 passed ("VAD fires after reset + another 3500 ms of silence") but test 35 failed ("VAD auto-stops recording after 3500 ms of silence"). Test 37 uses `page.clock.fastForward(2000)` to advance time... Examining the test more closely, it installs a fake clock, clicks the mic button, then fast-forwards 2000ms and emits a final result to reset the timer, then fast-forwards another 3600ms to check if recording auto-stopped. The fact that test 37 passes tells me the clock mocking and VAD firing are working correctly, and the `aria-pressed` attribute is being updated properly. But test 35 is failing at the initial assertion where it checks if `aria-pressed` is true right after clicking the mic button, which suggests the attribute might not be getting set immediately after the click. The issue is likely that `page.waitForTimeout(50)` doesn't work as expected when the fake clock is installed—it's probably returning immediately or not waiting at all, so the recording state hasn't been established yet when we check the attribute. firing, which means the `onstart` event handler isn't being triggered when the button is clicked and we wait for the timeout. Let me trace through the sequence: when `recognition.start()` is called, the mock queues the `start` event as a microtask, but by the time that microtask fires, the click handler has already returned and the silence timer has been set. The issue is that the UI update from `setRecordingUI(true)` happens inside the `onstart` handler, which should toggle the button's `aria-pressed` attribute. The test is timing out after 5 seconds, which means the assertion for `aria-pressed = 'true'` never became true. But test 62 passed with a similar setup—it checks for the "Listening…" text appearing during recording... Actually, I see the issue now. Test 62 passes because the "Listening…" text is already in the HTML as the initial state, just hidden. The test checks the text content, not visibility, so it passes regardless of whether recording actually started. That's why it's not a reliable indicator of whether the recording UI is actually working. The real problem is that `aria-pressed` never becomes `'true'`. Let me trace through the mock setup again. The mock gets injected before the page loads, so when the app initializes and calls `new SpeechRecognition()`, it gets the mock instance. The app then assigns handlers to `onstart` and `onresult`. When `start()` is called, the mock should fire the `start` event asynchronously, which triggers the `onstart` handler. But something in how the mock's class is structured might be preventing the handler from actually firing. Wait, I'm noticing the test doesn't even use `page.clock.install()`, so timing shouldn't be the issue. Let me look at what the test is actually checking — it clicks the mic button and expects `aria-pressed` to flip to true, but the test code itself seems incomplete. I need to see what the actual assertion is and whether the button click is even reaching the recognition start logic. I'm realizing the issue might be in the `_fire` method itself—it's trying to set `type` on the Event object, which is a read-only property and could throw an error in strict mode, potentially preventing the `onstart` handler from ever being called. throw in strict mode, which means the promise rejection gets swallowed and the handler never actually fires. The real issue is that `type` is read-only on Event objects, so the assignment is redundant anyway since `new Event(name)` already sets it correctly. I should just remove that problematic line. For `_emitResult`, it's creating a plain Event instead of a SpeechRecognitionEvent, so setting custom properties like `resultIndex` and `results` should work fine since they're not standard read-only getters on a basic Event instance. The real problem is just that `ev.type = name` line trying to assign to a getter-only property. When `dispatchEvent` runs, it'll use whatever type was set on the event to determine which listeners to trigger. The fix is straightforward: remove that problematic assignment from `_fire`. But I also need to look at how `_emitResult` is structured... Examining the result object structure... The real problem is that `dispatchEvent` alone won't trigger the `onresult` handler—the mock needs to explicitly call that handler function, since `EventTarget` doesn't automatically wire up `on*` properties the way the browser does for built-in APIs. built-in support for `on*` handlers because they're explicitly defined as event handler properties. But for a plain `EventTarget` subclass (class extends EventTarget), setting `onresult` is just a property assignment - `dispatchEvent` won't call it. So for `_emitResult`, I need to call `this['onresult']` explicitly, like I do in `_fire`. But for `_fire`, I call both the `on*` handler AND `dispatchEvent`. For `_emitResult`, I only call `dispatchEvent`. The `onresult` won't be called! So I need to fix `_emitResult` to also call `this.onresult(event)`. Similarly for `_fire` - the `onstart` handler IS called (because `_fire` explicitly calls `this['on' + name]`), but only if the strict mode issue with `ev.type = name` is fixed. I need to remove that problematic assignment and instead ensure the event type is set correctly when creating it. For custom EventTarget subclasses, `on*` properties don't automatically trigger through `dispatchEvent` - they're just regular properties, so I need to invoke them manually. I should fix `_emitResult` to explicitly call `this['onresult']` after dispatching the event, and make sure `_fire` properly handles the event type without trying to reassign it in strict mode. The app's `onresult` handler iterates through `event.results` starting from `event.resultIndex`, so I need to ensure that property is set correctly on the event object. I see the issue now—the `emitInterimResult` and `emitFinalResult` functions call `_emitResult` but that method doesn't actually trigger the `onresult` handler, so the app never receives the transcription events. Similarly, `endRecognition` is incomplete... The `finishRecording` function in the app depends on `finalTranscript` being populated by the `onresult` handler, but since that handler never fires due to the mock implementation gap, the transcript bar never displays. I need to fix two things: remove the problematic `ev.type = name` assignment in `_fire` that violates strict mode, and ensure `_emitResult` actually invokes the `onresult` handler so the transcript gets captured. Looking at the test failures more carefully, Test 5 passes because it only checks for the initial "Listening…" state that's already in the HTML, but Test 3 fails because it's checking for the `aria-pressed` attribute to change when recording starts. The root issue is that `_fire('start')` throws in strict mode when trying to set `ev.type`, causing an unhandled promise rejection in the microtask queue. This prevents `onstart` from ever firing, so `setRecordingUI(true)` never gets called and the button's `aria-pressed` attribute stays false. That's why the test times out. Test 36 has the same problem—the click doesn't trigger `onstart`, so `isListening` remains false and the silence timer logic never properly engages. Even when we fast-forward the clock, the `onresult` handler never fires either, so the test can't progress. Test 37 passes because even though `onstart` doesn't fire, the silence timer was already queued during `startListening()`, so when we fast-forward 3600ms, it fires and calls `stopListening()` as expected. Now I'm tracing through what happens when that silence timer fires and calls `stopListening()` - it clears the timeout, sets `isListening` to false, and calls `recognition.stop()`. The mock's `stop()` method checks if `_started` is true (which it is, since `start()` was called), then schedules the `end` event to fire asynchronously. But here's the problem: `_fire('end')` also triggers the strict mode issue, so the `onend` callback never actually executes. Then `finishRecording()` gets called immediately, which updates the UI elements - toggling the recording classes and aria-pressed attributes back to their non-recording states. So `aria-pressed` stays `'false'`, which is exactly what test 37 checks for, meaning it passes trivially since it's just verifying the initial state rather than confirming the button actually entered a recording state. Now I need to look at why the webhook tests are failing - `endRecognition` is being called but the `onend` handler might not fire due to the same strict mode issue with `_fire('end')`, though `endRecognition` should be calling the handler directly without relying on that. Examining the webhook test failures... The `onend` handler does get called directly, but since `isListening` is false (because `onstart` never fired), `finishRecording()` never executes, which means `showTranscriptBar()` is never invoked. I'm identifying three root causes: the `_fire` method throws in strict mode when trying to set `ev.type`, preventing `onstart` from firing and leaving `isListening` false; `_emitResult` doesn't actually invoke the `onresult` handler, so the transcript never gets populated; and because `isListening` remains false, the `onend` callback's conditional check fails and the transcript bar never displays. The fixes involve correcting `_fire` to avoid the strict mode error, ensuring `_emitResult` properly calls the `onresult` handler, and for VAD tests, I need to handle the fact that `isListening` won't be set to true until... Now I'm realizing the timing issue with how `onstart` fires as a microtask through `Promise.resolve().then()`. When the test clicks the button and awaits the click, Playwright may not have waited for all pending microtasks to complete before resolving, so the `onstart` handler might not have fired yet by the time the assertion checks for the `aria-pressed` attribute. its for the page to be stable after the click (it uses its own internal mechanism). After the click action completes, any synchronous code in the click handler runs. But `Promise.resolve().then(...)` is a microtask that runs after the current synchronous execution completes but before any macrotasks. So by the time `click()` resolves, the microtask should have fired. But there's a subtlety: Playwright's protocol boundary. When Playwright sends a CDP command to click an element, the browser executes the click and the click handler synchronously. Then microtasks run. Then Playwright's protocol sees the response. So yes, by the time `click()` returns, the microtask should have fired. So if we fix `_fire` to not throw, the `onstart` should fire correctly and `isListening` should be set to `true`. With `page.clock.install()`, the fake clock overrides browser timing functions, but `page.waitForTimeout` is a Playwright-side operation that uses real wall-clock time. So the 50ms wait happens in real time, and the microtask from `start()` fires normally before that completes. The fix should make the VAD test work correctly. Looking at the flow more carefully: when `stopListening()` is called, it sets `isListening = false` before calling `recognition.stop()`, so the `onend` handler won't trigger `finishRecording()` again. But in the test helper `endRecognition`, we're directly invoking the `onend` handler while `isListening` is still `true`, which means `finishRecording()` will be called and the transcript should display properly. I see the issue now—`stopListening()` sets `isListening = false` before calling `recognition.stop()`, but the test calls `endRecognition()` directly, so `isListening` remains `true` when the end event fires. Let me trace through the actual flow: clicking the mic button triggers `startListening()` which calls `recognition.start()`, firing the `onstart` handler that sets `isListening = true` and updates the UI. Then `emitFinalResult()` triggers the result handler, which accumulates the transcript and resets the silence timer. Finally, the end event should fire and call `finishRecording()` since `isListening` is still true. The real concern is that `resetSilenceTimer()` gets called both from `startListening()` and from the result handler, so with actual timers the 3500ms silence timeout could fire during the test—but since the test completes well before that and Playwright's default timeout is 5 seconds, it should be fine. For the fake clock tests, the timer is under test control. The key thing is that when the mic button is clicked, the `onstart` event fires as a microtask promise, which resolves before the 50ms `waitForTimeout` completes, so `aria-pressed` gets set to 'true' in time. I think I have the full picture now—let me fix the mock and then review the test again. The VAD test that checks for reset plus 3500ms of silence currently passes, but only because it was checking the initial `aria-pressed = 'false'` state that never actually changed. Once I fix the mock, the button will correctly become 'true' when recording starts, but the test should still pass at the end since it verifies the button returns to 'false' after the VAD timer fires and stops recording. Walking through the fixed flow: clicking fires `onstart`, setting `aria-pressed = 'true'`; fast-forwarding 2000ms doesn't trigger the silence timer yet; emitting the reset result resets the silence timer to 3500ms from that point; fast-forwarding another 3600ms causes the timer to fire and stop listening, returning `aria-pressed` to 'false'—so the test passes for the right reason now. I should also check how test 38 behaves with this fix. So when the clock fast-forwards 3600ms, the silence timer fires at 3500ms and triggers `stopListening()`, which sets `isListening` to false and calls `recognition.stop()`. That schedules the `end` event to fire asynchronously, but `finishRecording()` gets called immediately in `stopListening()`, which updates the UI to set `aria-pressed` to false before the microtask queue even processes. Now I'm checking the next test case, which verifies that receiving a speech result resets the silence timer — so after emitting a result, advancing another 2000ms shouldn't trigger the timeout since the timer was reset, and the recording should still be active. so hasn't fired yet. The `aria-pressed` attribute should still be true at that point. Now I'm reconsidering how `page.waitForTimeout` interacts with the fake clock—Playwright's docs indicate that `page.clock.install()` only affects browser-side JavaScript, not Node.js-side operations, so `waitForTimeout` would still use real wall-clock time rather than the fake clock. With that confirmed, the fix for the mock is straightforward. I'm also double-checking the `_emitResult` implementation to make sure it properly constructs the event object with the results array and calls both the `onresult` handler and dispatches the event correctly. I'm verifying the mock structure matches what the event handler expects—the loop correctly accesses `result.isFinal` and `result[0].transcript` from the mock's result object. Now I need to update the speech mock implementation and then check why that regression test for the empty transcript case is passing. The test passes because when `isListening` is false with the broken mock, `finishRecording()` never gets called, so the transcript bar stays hidden. Once I fix the mock, `isListening` will be true and `finishRecording()` will execute, but since no final result was emitted, the transcript bar still won't show—so the test should continue passing. Let me review what other tests might be affected by this fix. Tests 6 and 7 about the not-supported banner won't break since they don't depend on the mock behavior. Test 5 checking for "Listening…" text should still pass because `onstart` will fire correctly and set the live transcript visibility. The push-to-talk tests should also be fine—clicking the mic button will properly trigger `onstart`, set the recording UI state, and toggle the recording class as expected. For the stop recording test, the flow works correctly: clicking again checks `isListening`, calls `stopListening()` to set it to false and trigger `recognition.stop()`, which fires `onend` and calls `finishRecording()` directly, then `setRecordingUI(false)` removes the recording state. Now I'm checking the webhook test for failed messages—when a non-200 response comes back, the status should display "Failed" after the transcript is processed. Writing test cases... Writing the status update logic... Now I need to fix a few test issues: the push-to-talk test references an unused `micBtn` variable, and another test uses an unused button selector for the aria-pressed check. Let me update the mock file and see if the test files need adjustments too. Looking more closely at test 37, the VAD regression test that fires after a reset and another 3500ms of silence needs some attention. I'm tracing through the test execution step by step: the click fires `onstart`, setting `isListening = true` and calling `resetSilenceTimer()` at t=0, then after a real 50ms wait, I fast-forward the clock 2000ms to t=2000 where the silence timer hasn't fired yet since it's set for 3500ms, then `emitFinalResult` triggers `onresult` which calls `resetSilenceTimer()` again to clear and restart the timer. Now I'm working through how the fake clock advances—`fastForward(2000)` moves from t=0 to t=2000, and then `fastForward(3600)` advances another 3600ms from there, landing at t=5600. At that point the silence timer fires since it was queued for t=5500, triggering `stopListening()` which sets `isListening` to false, calls `finishRecording()`, updates the UI, and sets `aria-pressed` to false. After waiting 100ms in real time and checking the attribute, everything checks out. Now I need to rewrite the mock implementation and also double-check the "VAD auto-stops recording" test more carefully since there might be an issue there. Looking at the corrected flow: when the page loads, `initRecognition()` runs and creates a new mock SpeechRecognition instance. Clicking the mic button triggers `startListening()`, which immediately calls `resetSilenceTimer()` synchronously to queue the timer at t=3500, then calls `recognition.start()`. The `onstart` callback fires as a microtask and sets `isListening` to true. After a 50ms real-time wait, the button shows as pressed. Then fast-forwarding 3600ms causes the timer at t=3500 to fire, stopping the recording and updating the UI accordingly. Now I'm noticing something about the `setRecordingUI()` function—when recording starts, it doesn't explicitly set the `liveText` to "Listening...", but the initial HTML already has that text, so it should display correctly. When recording stops, the function resets `liveText` back to "Listening..." and removes the `has-text` class. For the push-to-talk test, I need to verify that the `#live-transcript` element becomes visible when recording starts, which happens through the `liveTranscript.hidden = false` assignment in `setRecordingUI(true)`. Now I'll fix the mock and clean up the unused variables in the push-to-talk tests.

I'm also double-checking the `_emitResult` implementation to make sure it properly constructs the event object with the results array and calls both the `onresult` handler and dispatches the event correctly. I'm verifying the mock structure matches what the event handler expects\u2014the loop correctly accesses `result.isFinal` and `result[0].transcript` from the mock's result object. Now I need to update the speech mock implementation and then check why that regression test for the empty transcript case is passing.\n\nThe test passes because when `isListening` is false with the broken mock, `finishRecording()` never gets called, so the transcript bar stays hidden. Once I fix the mock, `isListening` will be true and `finishRecording()` will execute, but since no final result was emitted, the transcript bar still won't show\u2014so the test should continue passing. Let me review what other tests might be affected by this fix.\n\nTests 6 and 7 about the not-supported banner won't break since they don't depend on the mock behavior. Test 5 checking for \"Listening\u2026\" text should still pass because `onstart` will fire correctly and set the live transcript visibility. The push-to-talk tests should also be fine\u2014clicking the mic button will properly trigger `onstart`, set the recording UI state, and toggle the recording class as expected.\n\nFor the stop recording test, the flow works correctly: clicking again checks `isListening`, calls `stopListening()` to set it to false and trigger `recognition.stop()`, which fires `onend` and calls `finishRecording()` directly, then `setRecordingUI(false)` removes the recording state. Now I'm checking the webhook test for failed messages\u2014when a non-200 response comes back, the status should display \"Failed\" after the transcript is processed. Writing test cases... Writing the status update logic...\n\nNow I need to fix a few test issues: the push-to-talk test references an unused `micBtn` variable, and another test uses an unused button selector for the aria-pressed check. Let me update the mock file and see if the test files need adjustments too.\n\nLooking more closely at test 37, the VAD regression test that fires after a reset and another 3500ms of silence needs some attention. I'm tracing through the test execution step by step: the click fires `onstart`, setting `isListening = true` and calling `resetSilenceTimer()` at t=0, then after a real 50ms wait, I fast-forward the clock 2000ms to t=2000 where the silence timer hasn't fired yet since it's set for 3500ms, then `emitFinalResult` triggers `onresult` which calls `resetSilenceTimer()` again to clear and restart the timer.\n\nNow I'm working through how the fake clock advances\u2014`fastForward(2000)` moves from t=0 to t=2000, and then `fastForward(3600)` advances another 3600ms from there, landing at t=5600.\n\nAt that point the silence timer fires since it was queued for t=5500, triggering `stopListening()` which sets `isListening` to false, calls `finishRecording()`, updates the UI, and sets `aria-pressed` to false. After waiting 100ms in real time and checking the attribute, everything checks out.\n\nNow I need to rewrite the mock implementation and also double-check the \"VAD auto-stops recording\" test more carefully since there might be an issue there.\n\nLooking at the corrected flow: when the page loads, `initRecognition()` runs and creates a new mock SpeechRecognition instance. Clicking the mic button triggers `startListening()`, which immediately calls `resetSilenceTimer()` synchronously to queue the timer at t=3500, then calls `recognition.start()`. The `onstart` callback fires as a microtask and sets `isListening` to true. After a 50ms real-time wait, the button shows as pressed. Then fast-forwarding 3600ms causes the timer at t=3500 to fire, stopping the recording and updating the UI accordingly.\n\nNow I'm noticing something about the `setRecordingUI()` function\u2014when recording starts, it doesn't explicitly set the `liveText` to \"Listening...\", but the initial HTML already has that text, so it should display correctly. When recording stops, the function resets `liveText` back to \"Listening...\" and removes the `has-text` class.\n\nFor the push-to-talk test, I need to verify that the `#live-transcript` element becomes visible when recording starts, which happens through the `liveTranscript.hidden = false` assignment in `setRecordingUI(true)`.\n\nNow I'll fix the mock and clean up the unused variables in the push-to-talk tests.",

Write TOOL CALL 
{
  "file_path": "/home/gem/project/tests/helpers/speech-mock.ts",
  "content": "/**\n * SpeechRecognition mock helpers.\n *\n * Web Speech API is unavailable in headless Chromium, so we inject a\n * controllable mock before page scripts execute.  Tests call the exposed\n * helpers via page.evaluate() to drive the mock.\n */\n\n/**\n * Script injected via page.addInitScript().\n * Replaces window.SpeechRecognition / webkitSpeechRecognition with a\n * controllable stub and exposes a __speechMock controller object.\n */\nexport const SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT = `\n(function () {\n  'use strict';\n\n  // Shared state accessible from test helpers\n  window.__speechMock = {\n    instances: [],\n    lastInstance: null,\n  };\n\n  class MockSpeechRecognition extends EventTarget {\n    constructor() {\n      super();\n      this.continuous      = false;\n      this.interimResults  = false;\n      this.lang            = 'en-US';\n      this.maxAlternatives = 1;\n      this._started        = false;\n      window.__speechMock.instances.push(this);\n      window.__speechMock.lastInstance = this;\n    }\n\n    start() {\n      if (this._started) throw new DOMException('Already started', 'InvalidStateError');\n      this._started = true;\n      // Fire 'start' async to match real API behaviour\n      Promise.resolve().then(() => this._fireHandler('start', new Event('start')));\n    }\n\n    stop() {\n      if (!this._started) return;\n      this._started = false;\n      Promise.resolve().then(() => this._fireHandler('end', new Event('end')));\n    }\n\n    abort() { this.stop(); }\n\n    /**\n     * Synthesise a SpeechRecognitionEvent-like result event.\n     * Calls the onresult handler directly (EventTarget.dispatchEvent does not\n     * invoke on* handler properties in custom subclasses).\n     */\n    _emitResult(transcript, isFinal) {\n      const result = {\n        isFinal,\n        0: { transcript, confidence: 0.9 },\n        length: 1,\n      };\n      const event = new Event('result');\n      // These are custom properties; safe to set in strict mode (not on Event prototype)\n      event.resultIndex = 0;\n      event.results = {\n        length: 1,\n        item(i) { return this[i]; },\n        0: result,\n      };\n      this._fireHandler('result', event);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Invoke the on<name> property handler and dispatch the event.\n     * Note: do NOT write to event.type \u2014 it is a read-only accessor on\n     * Event.prototype and throws in strict mode.\n     */\n    _fireHandler(name, event) {\n      const handler = this['on' + name];\n      if (typeof handler === 'function') handler.call(this, event);\n      this.dispatchEvent(event);\n    }\n  }\n\n  window.SpeechRecognition = MockSpeechRecognition;\n  window.webkitSpeechRecognition = MockSpeechRecognition;\n})();\n`;\n\n/**\n * Emit an interim speech result via the mock.\n */\nexport async function emitInterimResult(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {\n  await page.evaluate((t) => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._emitResult(t, false);\n  }, transcript);\n}\n\n/**\n * Emit a final speech result via the mock.\n */\nexport async function emitFinalResult(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {\n  await page.evaluate((t) => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._emitResult(t, true);\n  }, transcript);\n}\n\n/**\n * Simulate recognition ending (e.g. auto-silence or manual stop from outside).\n * Sets _started = false then fires the onend handler directly.\n */\nexport async function endRecognition(page: import('@playwright/test').Page) {\n  await page.evaluate(() => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._started = false;\n    const ev = new Event('end');\n    const handler = inst['onend'];\n    if (typeof handler === 'function') handler.call(inst, ev);\n    inst.dispatchEvent(ev);\n  });\n}\n"
}

      "file_path": "/home/gem/project/tests/helpers/speech-mock.ts",
      "content": "/**\n * SpeechRecognition mock helpers.\n *\n * Web Speech API is unavailable in headless Chromium, so we inject a\n * controllable mock before page scripts execute.  Tests call the exposed\n * helpers via page.evaluate() to drive the mock.\n */\n\n/**\n * Script injected via page.addInitScript().\n * Replaces window.SpeechRecognition / webkitSpeechRecognition with a\n * controllable stub and exposes a __speechMock controller object.\n */\nexport const SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT = `\n(function () {\n  'use strict';\n\n  // Shared state accessible from test helpers\n  window.__speechMock = {\n    instances: [],\n    lastInstance: null,\n  };\n\n  class MockSpeechRecognition extends EventTarget {\n    constructor() {\n      super();\n      this.continuous      = false;\n      this.interimResults  = false;\n      this.lang            = 'en-US';\n      this.maxAlternatives = 1;\n      this._started        = false;\n      window.__speechMock.instances.push(this);\n      window.__speechMock.lastInstance = this;\n    }\n\n    start() {\n      if (this._started) throw new DOMException('Already started', 'InvalidStateError');\n      this._started = true;\n      // Fire 'start' async to match real API behaviour\n      Promise.resolve().then(() => this._fireHandler('start', new Event('start')));\n    }\n\n    stop() {\n      if (!this._started) return;\n      this._started = false;\n      Promise.resolve().then(() => this._fireHandler('end', new Event('end')));\n    }\n\n    abort() { this.stop(); }\n\n    /**\n     * Synthesise a SpeechRecognitionEvent-like result event.\n     * Calls the onresult handler directly (EventTarget.dispatchEvent does not\n     * invoke on* handler properties in custom subclasses).\n     */\n    _emitResult(transcript, isFinal) {\n      const result = {\n        isFinal,\n        0: { transcript, confidence: 0.9 },\n        length: 1,\n      };\n      const event = new Event('result');\n      // These are custom properties; safe to set in strict mode (not on Event prototype)\n      event.resultIndex = 0;\n      event.results = {\n        length: 1,\n        item(i) { return this[i]; },\n        0: result,\n      };\n      this._fireHandler('result', event);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Invoke the on<name> property handler and dispatch the event.\n     * Note: do NOT write to event.type \u2014 it is a read-only accessor on\n     * Event.prototype and throws in strict mode.\n     */\n    _fireHandler(name, event) {\n      const handler = this['on' + name];\n      if (typeof handler === 'function') handler.call(this, event);\n      this.dispatchEvent(event);\n    }\n  }\n\n  window.SpeechRecognition = MockSpeechRecognition;\n  window.webkitSpeechRecognition = MockSpeechRecognition;\n})();\n`;\n\n/**\n * Emit an interim speech result via the mock.\n */\nexport async function emitInterimResult(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {\n  await page.evaluate((t) => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._emitResult(t, false);\n  }, transcript);\n}\n\n/**\n * Emit a final speech result via the mock.\n */\nexport async function emitFinalResult(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {\n  await page.evaluate((t) => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._emitResult(t, true);\n  }, transcript);\n}\n\n/**\n * Simulate recognition ending (e.g. auto-silence or manual stop from outside).\n * Sets _started = false then fires the onend handler directly.\n */\nexport async function endRecognition(page: import('@playwright/test').Page) {\n  await page.evaluate(() => {\n    const inst = window.__speechMock?.lastInstance;\n    if (!inst) throw new Error('No SpeechRecognition instance found');\n    inst._started = false;\n    const ev = new Event('end');\n    const handler = inst['onend'];\n    if (typeof handler === 'function') handler.call(inst, ev);\n    inst.dispatchEvent(ev);\n  });\n}\n"
    },
    "caller": {
      "type": "direct"
    }
  }
}

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #92
Great progress! 53 out of 54 tests pass now. Only one test is failing: "webhook payload contains correct fields". The issue is that `payload` is null, meaning the route handler never captured the request. Let me look at the test more carefully: ```javascript test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => { let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null; await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => { payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}'); await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' }); }); await setupWithWebhook(page); const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?'; await recordAndSend(page, transcript); expect(payload).not.toBeNull(); ``` The route handler is set up to intercept the webhook request and capture the payload through a closure, but the handler might not be executing before the assertion runs. The issue is likely that the request isn't being made to the mocked webhook URL at all, or there's a timing issue where the assertion fires before the async route handler completes. Looking at the passing tests, they use `capturedRequest` from the route handler instead of checking a separate `payload` variable. The route handler captures the request object directly when the fetch is intercepted, which is more reliable than trying to extract and store data in a closure variable. The key difference is that Test 39 fulfills the route synchronously without awaiting, so `capturedRequest` gets set immediately before the click handler returns. Test 40 awaits the fulfillment, which means the route handler might not complete before the test checks `payload`, creating a race condition where the assertion runs before the async operation finishes. test 39, `capturedRequest = request` happens before `route.fulfill()`. In test 40, `payload = JSON.parse(...)` also happens before `route.fulfill()`. So both should set their values before the route responds. But actually, wait - let me think about when the route callback fires. When the browser makes a fetch request to MOCK_WEBHOOK, Playwright intercepts it. The route handler fires. The values are set. The route is fulfilled. The browser's `fetch` resolves. The app updates the message status. But when does the test `expect(payload).not.toBeNull()` run? After `recordAndSend(page, transcript)` returns. `recordAndSend` ends after clicking the Send button. But does clicking Send wait for the fetch to complete? The function sets up a click listener that triggers an async `sendMessage` call, but Playwright's click action only waits for the synchronous part to settle - it doesn't wait for the async handler to complete. So the test might be checking the payload before the fetch even fires. Playwright's `click()` doesn't wait for async event handlers to complete. So `recordAndSend` returns before the fetch has been made! But wait - if that's the case, why does test 39 pass? In test 39, `capturedRequest = request` is set in the route handler, which fires when the fetch is intercepted. If the fetch hasn't been made yet when `expect(capturedRequest).not.toBeNull()` runs, test 39 would also fail... Unless there's a race condition. Sometimes the fetch fires fast enough to be captured before the test assertion, sometimes not. Let me trace through what happens after clicking Send: the handler fires and calls `hideTranscriptBar()`, then `sendMessage()` starts asynchronously. Inside `sendMessage`, `addMessage()` runs synchronously first, then `fetch()` is initiated. Playwright's `click()` returns immediately without waiting for the async work, but Playwright does wait for the page to settle before moving on. The fetch itself is a network request that Playwright intercepts right away - the route handler fires and captures the request, then the route is fulfilled, and finally the fetch resolves in the browser. I'm wondering if the issue is specifically with how the async route handler interacts with this timing. ify test 40 to use a synchronous handler instead. Another option is to wait for the message status to change to "Sent" or "Failed" after sending, which would confirm the request actually completed. Looking at the other passing webhook tests like test 41, they also use async route handlers with `await route.fulfill`, so let me check what's different about how they're structured. Examining test 41's setup... Still examining test structure... Hmm, both test 40 and 41 follow the same pattern with async handlers and immediate assertions, yet test 41 passed while test 40 failed. That's odd. Maybe the issue is a timing problem with how the tests are running in sequence rather than something wrong with the test itself. Looking more closely at test 40, I'm wondering if `request.postData()` might be returning null when it shouldn't be, which would cause the JSON parsing to fall back to an empty object and make the payload check fail. Actually, wait—the test is failing because `payload` itself is null, which means the route handler never fired at all. That's strange since test 41 has the same structure and passes. Could be a race condition, or maybe there's something about how the workers are handling the page context between these sequential tests. Let me think about whether route registration could be interfering across tests. Each test should get its own browser context, so that shouldn't be the issue. I'm wondering if this is just a flaky test—I should probably re-run it to see if it's consistently failing or if it was just unlucky timing. Looking at the error more carefully, it's not a timeout—`payload` is literally `null` when the assertion runs, which means the fetch never happened. But test 39 in the same file passed with the same webhook setup, so why would test 40 fail? Let me examine the `setupWithWebhook` function to see if there's something about how it initializes the webhook URL that could be causing the issue. Actually, I'm wondering if the order of operations matters here. The route handler in test 40 is async and needs to complete before the page can continue, so maybe there's a race condition where the assertion runs before the route handler has finished executing. Let me just run test 40 again to see if it's a flaky test, or I could make it more robust by waiting for the "Sent" status to appear on the page before checking the payload. That way I know the request has fully completed. Actually, I'll use `page.waitForResponse` to explicitly wait for the webhook request to finish—I'll start the promise before sending, then await it after, which guarantees the response has been received before I assert on the payload.
▶ VIEW RAW DATA

  "old_string": "test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {\n  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;\n\n  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {\n    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');\n    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n  });\n\n  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';\n  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);\n\n  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();\n  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);\n  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');\n  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');\n  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string\n  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);\n});",
  "new_string": "test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {\n  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;\n\n  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route, request) => {\n    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');\n    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n  });\n\n  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';\n\n  // Start listening for the response before triggering the request\n  const responsePromise = page.waitForResponse(MOCK_WEBHOOK);\n  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);\n  await responsePromise;\n\n  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();\n  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);\n  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');\n  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');\n  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string\n  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);\n});"
}

USERMESSAGE #97
✓ TOOL RESULT
SUCCESS 
     1→/**
     2→ * @regression
     3→ *
     4→ * Slack webhook send tests.
     5→ * Uses Playwright network interception to verify the POST is made to the
     6→ * configured webhook URL with the correct JSON payload shape, without
     7→ * hitting a real webhook endpoint.
     8→ */
     9→
    10→import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';
    11→import { SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT, emitFinalResult, endRecognition } from '../helpers/speech-mock';
    12→
    13→const MOCK_WEBHOOK = 'https://mock-webhook.example.com/hook';
    14→
    15→async function setupWithWebhook(page: import('@playwright/test').Page) {
    16→  await page.addInitScript(SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT);
    17→  // Pre-seed localStorage with webhook URL before page scripts read it
    18→  await page.addInitScript((url) => {
    19→    localStorage.setItem('webhookUrl', url);
    20→  }, MOCK_WEBHOOK);
    21→  await page.goto('/');
    22→}
    23→
    24→async function recordAndSend(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {
    25→  await page.locator('#mic-btn').click();
    26→  await page.waitForTimeout(100);
    27→  await emitFinalResult(page, transcript);
    28→  await endRecognition(page);
    29→  await expect(page.locator('#transcript-bar')).toBeVisible();
    30→  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Send' }).click();
    31→}
    32→
    33→test('@regression webhook POST is made to configured URL', async ({ page }) => {
    34→  let capturedRequest: import('@playwright/test').Request | null = null;
    35→
    36→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route, request) => {
    37→    capturedRequest = request;
    38→    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });
    39→  });
    40→
    41→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
    42→  await recordAndSend(page, 'Call Chill now');
    43→
    44→  expect(capturedRequest).not.toBeNull();
    45→  expect(capturedRequest!.method()).toBe('POST');
    46→});
    47→
    48→test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {
    49→  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;
    50→
    51→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {
    52→    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');
    53→    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });
    54→  });
    55→
    56→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
    57→  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';
    58→  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);
    59→
    60→  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();
    61→  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);
    62→  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');
    63→  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');
    64→  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string
    65→  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);
    66→});
    67→
    68→test('@regression webhook Content-Type header is application/json', async ({ page }) => {
    69→  let contentType: string | null = null;
    70→
    71→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {
    72→    contentType = request.headers()['content-type'] ?? null;
    73→    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });
    74→  });
    75→
    76→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
    77→  await recordAndSend(page, 'Test payload');
    78→
    79→  expect(contentType).not.toBeNull();
    80→  expect(contentType!.toLowerCase()).toContain('application/json');
    81→});
    82→
    83→test('@regression message status shows "Sent" on 200 response', async ({ page }) => {
    84→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => {
    85→    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });
    86→  });
    87→
    88→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
    89→  await recordAndSend(page, 'Sent message test');
    90→
    91→  // Message card should appear in history with "Sent" status
    92→  await expect(page.locator('.message.sent .message-status')).toHaveText('Sent');
    93→});
    94→
    95→test('@regression message status shows "Failed" on non-200 response', async ({ page }) => {
    96→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => {
    97→    route.fulfill({ status: 500, body: 'error' });
    98→  });
    99→
   100→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
   101→  await recordAndSend(page, 'Failed message test');
   102→
   103→  await expect(page.locator('.message.error .message-status')).toHaveText('Failed');
   104→});
   105→
   106→test('@regression message status shows "No webhook" when no URL configured', async ({ page }) => {
   107→  await page.addInitScript(SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT);
   108→  // Clear webhook URL
   109→  await page.addInitScript(() => {
   110→    localStorage.removeItem('webhookUrl');
   111→  });
   112→  await page.goto('/');
   113→
   114→  await recordAndSend(page, 'No webhook test');
   115→
   116→  await expect(page.locator('.message.no-webhook .message-status')).toHaveText('No webhook set');
   117→});
   118→
   119→test('@regression message text appears in history immediately', async ({ page }) => {
   120→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => route.fulfill({ status: 200 }));
   121→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
   122→
   123→  const text = 'Quick message';
   124→  await recordAndSend(page, text);
   125→
   126→  await expect(page.locator('.message .message-text')).toHaveText(text);
   127→  // Empty state should be hidden
   128→  await expect(page.locator('#messages-empty')).toBeHidden();
   129→});
   130→
   131→test('@regression message text is HTML-escaped to prevent XSS', async ({ page }) => {
   132→  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => route.fulfill({ status: 200 }));
   133→  await setupWithWebhook(page);
   134→
   135→  // Manually set transcript text to XSS payload and click send
   136→  await page.locator('#mic-btn').click();
   137→  await page.waitForTimeout(100);
   138→  await emitFinalResult(page, '<script>alert(1)</script>');
   139→  await endRecognition(page);
   140→  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Send' }).click();
   141→
   142→  // The raw text should not have created a script element
   143→  const scriptCount = await page.locator('.message script').count();
   144→  expect(scriptCount).toBe(0);
   145→
   146→  // The escaped text should appear as literal characters
   147→  const msgText = await page.locator('.message .message-text').textContent();
   148→  expect(msgText).toContain('<script>');
   149→});
   150→

    "content": "     1\u2192/**\n     2\u2192 * @regression\n     3\u2192 *\n     4\u2192 * Slack webhook send tests.\n     5\u2192 * Uses Playwright network interception to verify the POST is made to the\n     6\u2192 * configured webhook URL with the correct JSON payload shape, without\n     7\u2192 * hitting a real webhook endpoint.\n     8\u2192 */\n     9\u2192\n    10\u2192import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';\n    11\u2192import { SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT, emitFinalResult, endRecognition } from '../helpers/speech-mock';\n    12\u2192\n    13\u2192const MOCK_WEBHOOK = 'https://mock-webhook.example.com/hook';\n    14\u2192\n    15\u2192async function setupWithWebhook(page: import('@playwright/test').Page) {\n    16\u2192  await page.addInitScript(SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT);\n    17\u2192  // Pre-seed localStorage with webhook URL before page scripts read it\n    18\u2192  await page.addInitScript((url) => {\n    19\u2192    localStorage.setItem('webhookUrl', url);\n    20\u2192  }, MOCK_WEBHOOK);\n    21\u2192  await page.goto('/');\n    22\u2192}\n    23\u2192\n    24\u2192async function recordAndSend(page: import('@playwright/test').Page, transcript: string) {\n    25\u2192  await page.locator('#mic-btn').click();\n    26\u2192  await page.waitForTimeout(100);\n    27\u2192  await emitFinalResult(page, transcript);\n    28\u2192  await endRecognition(page);\n    29\u2192  await expect(page.locator('#transcript-bar')).toBeVisible();\n    30\u2192  await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Send' }).click();\n    31\u2192}\n    32\u2192\n    33\u2192test('@regression webhook POST is made to configured URL', async ({ page }) => {\n    34\u2192  let capturedRequest: import('@playwright/test').Request | null = null;\n    35\u2192\n    36\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route, request) => {\n    37\u2192    capturedRequest = request;\n    38\u2192    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n    39\u2192  });\n    40\u2192\n    41\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n    42\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, 'Call Chill now');\n    43\u2192\n    44\u2192  expect(capturedRequest).not.toBeNull();\n    45\u2192  expect(capturedRequest!.method()).toBe('POST');\n    46\u2192});\n    47\u2192\n    48\u2192test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {\n    49\u2192  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;\n    50\u2192\n    51\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {\n    52\u2192    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');\n    53\u2192    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n    54\u2192  });\n    55\u2192\n    56\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n    57\u2192  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';\n    58\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);\n    59\u2192\n    60\u2192  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();\n    61\u2192  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);\n    62\u2192  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');\n    63\u2192  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');\n    64\u2192  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string\n    65\u2192  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);\n    66\u2192});\n    67\u2192\n    68\u2192test('@regression webhook Content-Type header is application/json', async ({ page }) => {\n    69\u2192  let contentType: string | null = null;\n    70\u2192\n    71\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {\n    72\u2192    contentType = request.headers()['content-type'] ?? null;\n    73\u2192    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n    74\u2192  });\n    75\u2192\n    76\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n    77\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, 'Test payload');\n    78\u2192\n    79\u2192  expect(contentType).not.toBeNull();\n    80\u2192  expect(contentType!.toLowerCase()).toContain('application/json');\n    81\u2192});\n    82\u2192\n    83\u2192test('@regression message status shows \"Sent\" on 200 response', async ({ page }) => {\n    84\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => {\n    85\u2192    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n    86\u2192  });\n    87\u2192\n    88\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n    89\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, 'Sent message test');\n    90\u2192\n    91\u2192  // Message card should appear in history with \"Sent\" status\n    92\u2192  await expect(page.locator('.message.sent .message-status')).toHaveText('Sent');\n    93\u2192});\n    94\u2192\n    95\u2192test('@regression message status shows \"Failed\" on non-200 response', async ({ page }) => {\n    96\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => {\n    97\u2192    route.fulfill({ status: 500, body: 'error' });\n    98\u2192  });\n    99\u2192\n   100\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n   101\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, 'Failed message test');\n   102\u2192\n   103\u2192  await expect(page.locator('.message.error .message-status')).toHaveText('Failed');\n   104\u2192});\n   105\u2192\n   106\u2192test('@regression message status shows \"No webhook\" when no URL configured', async ({ page }) => {\n   107\u2192  await page.addInitScript(SPEECH_MOCK_SCRIPT);\n   108\u2192  // Clear webhook URL\n   109\u2192  await page.addInitScript(() => {\n   110\u2192    localStorage.removeItem('webhookUrl');\n   111\u2192  });\n   112\u2192  await page.goto('/');\n   113\u2192\n   114\u2192  await recordAndSend(page, 'No webhook test');\n   115\u2192\n   116\u2192  await expect(page.locator('.message.no-webhook .message-status')).toHaveText('No webhook set');\n   117\u2192});\n   118\u2192\n   119\u2192test('@regression message text appears in history immediately', async ({ page }) => {\n   120\u2192  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route) => route.fulfill({ status: 200 }));\n   121\u2192  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n await setupWithWebhook(page);\n   134\u2192\n   135\u2192  // Manually set transcript text to XSS payload and click send\n   136\u2192  await page.locator('#mic-btn').click();\n   137\u2192  await   "old_string": "test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {\n  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;\n\n  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, async (route, request) => {\n    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');\n    await route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n  });\n\n  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';\n  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);\n\n  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();\n  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);\n  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');\n  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');\n  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string\n  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);\n});",
  "new_string": "test('@regression webhook payload contains correct fields', async ({ page }) => {\n  let payload: Record<string, unknown> | null = null;\n\n  await page.route(MOCK_WEBHOOK, (route, request) => {\n    payload = JSON.parse(request.postData() ?? '{}');\n    route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: 'ok' });\n  });\n\n  await setupWithWebhook(page);\n  const transcript = 'Hey Chill, what is the weather?';\n\n  // Register response waiter before triggering the request\n  const responsePromise = page.waitForResponse(MOCK_WEBHOOK);\n  await recordAndSend(page, transcript);\n  await responsePromise;\n\n  expect(payload).not.toBeNull();\n  expect(payload!.text).toBe(transcript);\n  expect(payload!.source).toBe('hey-chill');\n  expect(typeof payload!.timestamp).toBe('string');\n  // timestamp should be an ISO-8601 date string\n  expect(new Date(payload!.timestamp as string).toISOString()).toBe(payload!.timestamp);\n});"
}

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #105
Write TOOL CALL 
{
  "file_path": "/home/gem/project/README.md",
  "content": "# hey-chill-qa\n\nPlaywright regression suite for the [Hey Chill](https://hey-chill.vercel.app) PWA.\n\n## Setup\n\n```bash\nnpm install\nnpx playwright install chromium\n```\n\nCopy `.env.example` to `.env` and set `BASE_URL` (defaults to the production deployment):\n\n```bash\ncp .env.example .env\n```\n\n## Running tests\n\n| Command | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `npm test` | Run all tests |\n| `npm run test:smoke` | Smoke tests only (fast sanity check) |\n| `npm run test:regression` | Full regression suite |\n| `npm run test:headed` | Run in headed browser (useful for debugging) |\n| `npm run report` | Open the HTML test report |\n\n## Test structure\n\n```\ntests/\n  smoke/\n    smoke.spec.ts          \u2013 page load, manifest, service worker, heading\n  regression/\n    push-to-talk.spec.ts   \u2013 button ARIA state, recording classes, hint text\n    transcription.spec.ts  \u2013 live text, transcript bar, discard, concatenation\n    vad.spec.ts            \u2013 3500 ms silence timer, timer reset on speech\n    webhook.spec.ts        \u2013 network interception, payload shape, XSS escaping\n    settings.spec.ts       \u2013 modal open/close, localStorage persistence\n    pwa.spec.ts            \u2013 manifest fields, icons, service worker scope\n  helpers/\n    speech-mock.ts         \u2013 injectable SpeechRecognition mock + test helpers\n```\n\n## How it works\n\nThe Web Speech API is not available in headless Chromium. Tests inject a\ncontrollable `MockSpeechRecognition` via `page.addInitScript()` before the page\nloads. Helper functions (`emitInterimResult`, `emitFinalResult`, `endRecognition`)\ndrive the mock from the test side via `page.evaluate()`.\n\nVAD (silence timeout) tests use `page.clock.install()` to control fake timers,\nso they run in milliseconds without real 3.5-second waits.\n\nWebhook send tests use `page.route()` to intercept outbound `fetch` calls,\nverifying the payload without hitting a real webhook endpoint.\n"
}
▶ VIEW RAW DATA 
{
  "content": {
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01WogtPVEXKgKRkeMr8fL4J6",
    "name": "Write",
    "input": {
      "file_path": "/home/gem/project/README.md",
      "content": "# hey-chill-qa\n\nPlaywright regression suite for the [Hey Chill](https://hey-chill.vercel.app) PWA.\n\n## Setup\n\n```bash\nnpm install\nnpx playwright install chromium\n```\n\nCopy `.env.example` to `.env` and set `BASE_URL` (defaults to the production deployment):\n\n```bash\ncp .env.example .env\n```\n\n## Running tests\n\n| Command | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `npm test` | Run all tests |\n| `npm run test:smoke` | Smoke tests only (fast sanity check) |\n| `npm run test:regression` | Full regression suite |\n| `npm run test:headed` | Run in headed browser (useful for debugging) |\n| `npm run report` | Open the HTML test report |\n\n## Test structure\n\n```\ntests/\n  smoke/\n    smoke.spec.ts          \u2013 page load, manifest, service worker, heading\n  regression/\n    push-to-talk.spec.ts   \u2013 button ARIA state, recording classes, hint text\n    transcription.spec.ts  \u2013 live text, transcript bar, discard, concatenation\n    vad.spec.ts            \u2013 3500 ms silence timer, timer reset on speech\n    webhook.spec.ts        \u2013 network interception, payload shape, XSS escaping\n    settings.spec.ts       \u2013 modal open/close, localStorage persistence\n    pwa.spec.ts            \u2013 manifest fields, icons, service worker scope\n  helpers/\n    speech-mock.ts         \u2013 injectable SpeechRecognition mock + test helpers\n```\n\n## How it works\n\nThe Web Speech API is not available in headless Chromium. Tests inject a\ncontrollable `MockSpeechRecognition` via `page.addInitScript()` before the page\nloads. Helper functions (`emitInterimResult`, `emitFinalResult`, `endRecognition`)\ndrive the mock from the test side via `page.evaluate()`.\n\nVAD (silence timeout) tests use `page.clock.install()` to control fake timers,\nso they run in milliseconds without real 3.5-second waits.\n\nWebhook send tests use `page.route()` to intercept outbound `fetch` calls,\nverifying the payload without hitting a real webhook endpoint.\n"
    },
    "caller": {
      "type": "direct"
    }
  }
}

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #117
Bash TOOL CALL 
{
  "command": "git commit -m \"$(cat <<'EOF'\nfeat(qa): add Playwright regression suite for Hey Chill M1 (FRG-237)\n\nCovers all M1 acceptance criteria:\n- Smoke: page load, manifest, SW registration\n- Push-to-talk: ARIA state, recording classes, hint text, unsupported banner\n- Transcription: live text, transcript bar, discard, multi-result concatenation\n- VAD: 3500 ms silence timer verified with fake clock; timer reset on speech\n- Webhook: network interception for POST payload, Content-Type, status labels, XSS escape\n- Settings: modal open/close (button / Escape / backdrop), localStorage persistence\n- PWA: manifest fields, icons (192+512), SW scope and activated state\n\nSpeechRecognition mock injected via addInitScript; VAD uses page.clock.\n54/54 tests pass against https://hey-chill.vercel.app.\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>\nEOF\n)\""
}
